coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of quantum carbon dots into a PVP/ZnO hydrogel for use as an effective hexavalent chromium sensing platform
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2019 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Truskewycz, Adam
Beker, Sabrina A.
Ball, Andrew S.
Murdoch, Billy
Cole, Ivan
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogels offer a unique medium for sensing applications as they can draw upon the benefits of both solid and liquid sensing platforms. Incorporation of functionalised fluorescent nanoparticles within the hydrogel matrix results in a three-dimensional nanocomposite capable of interacting with water-soluble analytes, facilitating quantitative sensing applications. Here, we report the solvothermal synthesis of a novel Polyvinylpyrrolidone assembled hydrogel stabilised with zinc oxide quantum dots which does not require additional organic crosslinkers. Functionalization of these hydrogel with novel, brightly photoluminescent 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol quantum carbon dots (quantum yield of 62.5%) results in a composite capable of specific interaction with hexavalent chromium. Quantitative fluorescence quenching measurements of the hydrogel composite in the presence of hexavalent chromium shows a limit of detection of 1.2 μM Cr6+ which is below maximum allowable concentrations for drinking water. The hydrogel composite is cheap to manufacture and can be injected into 96 well plates for high throughput analysis of environmental water samples. These results are encouraging for the development of hydrogels and polymetric films to be used as novel fluorescent sensing platforms for environmental diagnostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Zinc oxide
Quantum dots
Sensing
Hexavalent chromium
Polyvinylpyrrolidone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water holding capacity and re-swelling capacity in saline solutions
Hexavalent chromium sensing in environmental water samples
Physical and chemical properties of hydrogel
Sensing of hexavalent chromium in environmental samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: 1. The authors acknowledge the facilities, and the scientific and technical assistance of the RMIT Microscopy & Microanalysis Facility (RMMF), a linked laboratory of Microscopy Australia. 2. The authors acknowledge the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for providing the PhD scholarship to Sabrina Beker (co-author). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Left to right) Hydrogel pellet prior to purification, hydrogel following removal of precursors, hydrogels intrinsic fluorescence, SEM image of hydrogel.
(A) Weight of hydrogel with and without absorbed water (B) Swelling of hydrogel over time with different NaCl concentrations. Error bars represent the standard error from triplicate measurements.
(A) TEM image of hydrogels nanofibrular matrix –insert shows the size distribution of ZnO quantum dots within the hydrogel matrix (B) Hydrogels XRD spectra.
TEM image of QCD’s synthesized hydrothermally with 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol and citric acid insert shows the size distribution of ZnO quantum dots within the hydrogel matrix.
(A) Excitation emission spectra of hydrogel (B) Excitation emission spectra of QCD -hydrogel.
(A) Metal ion selectivity of (A) hydrogel and (B) QCD-hydrogel-based on fluorescence profiles in the presence of different anions/cations (2.5 mM).

(A) Emission spectra of the QCD-Hydrogel in the presence of various concentrations of Cr6+ in river water (B) Absorbance profiles for the hydrogel before and after Cr6+ addition (1.25 mM) (C & D) Linearity plot of relative fluorescence quenching ratio with Cr6+ concentrations of 9.7–625 μM & 0.0–4.9 μM respectively.
XPS spectra of carbon in the hydrogel (A) before and (B) after saturation with Cr6+ (C) FTIR spectra of QCD’s in the presence of 1.25 mM Cr6+.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of quantum carbon dots into a PVP/ZnO hydrogel for use as an effective hexavalent chromium sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3083, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3083, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: RMIT Microscopy and Microanalysis Facility, Melbourne, Victoria, 3001, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: RMIT Microscopy and Microanalysis Facility, Melbourne, Victoria, 3001, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Manufacturing and Fabrication, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Manufacturing and Fabrication, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC, 3000, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3083, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogels offer a unique medium for sensing applications as they can draw upon the benefits of both solid and liquid sensing platforms. Incorporation of functionalised fluorescent nanoparticles within the hydrogel matrix results in a three-dimensional nanocomposite capable of interacting with water-soluble analytes, facilitating quantitative sensing applications. Here, we report the solvothermal synthesis of a novel Polyvinylpyrrolidone assembled hydrogel stabilised with zinc oxide quantum dots which does not require additional organic crosslinkers. Functionalization of these hydrogel with novel, brightly photoluminescent 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol quantum carbon dots (quantum yield of 62.5%) results in a composite capable of specific interaction with hexavalent chromium. Quantitative fluorescence quenching measurements of the hydrogel composite in the presence of hexavalent chromium shows a limit of detection of 1.2 μM Cr6+ which is below maximum allowable concentrations for drinking water. The hydrogel composite is cheap to manufacture and can be injected into 96 well plates for high throughput analysis of environmental water samples. These results are encouraging for the development of hydrogels and polymetric films to be used as novel fluorescent sensing platforms for environmental diagnostic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels have been used for a number of different applications including medical drug delivery, water crystals for agricultural use, pollutant absorbents, coal de-watering agents, artificial snow, food additives, tissue engineering, diagnostics and separation of biomolecules; however, only recently have their potential for environmental sensing been explored [1]. Their capacity to absorb water soluble compounds makes them particularly useful for facilitating interactions between ionic species and the functional groups throughout their matrix. Moreover, their potential for the incorporation of various receptors and functional nanomaterials into the matrix allows them to possess a multitude of enhancements [2–4]. Hydrogels have previously been functionalised with peptides for tissue regeneration, pollution-reducing nanoparticles, fluorescent dyes, conductive and absorptive agents and nanomaterials for slow release of therapeutics.

The use of modified hydrogels as functional sensing systems is a new direction being explored for sensing applications with the potential to replace liquid and solid platforms as it incorporates the benefits of both. Solid sensing platforms suffer from particle aggregation, un-even analyte adsorption and altered fluorescence outputs. In addition, analyte recognition is only effective at the surface interface. Liquid platforms suffer from shorter shelf life and often require scientific intervention and are difficult to implement in field-based settings. As hydrogels possess a 3-dimentional matrix, interactions between analyte and receptor can occur over multiple interfaces and allow the transport of water soluble components throughout the matrices. The quasi solid polymer matrix can be integrated with fluorescent recognition molecules which can intimately interact with water and other ionic constituents. This binding interaction can result in a measurable change in fluorescence and concentrations of analyte can be determined.

Quantum carbon dots (QCD) are one example of fluorescent recognition entities which can be immobilised into hydrogel matrices for sensing applications owing to their small size (<10 nm), high surface area to volume ratio, excellent photostability and bright photoluminescence [5]. Quantum carbon dots are uncomplicated to functionalise, possess strong tuneable fluorescence properties following functionalization, may be soluble in water, are biocompatible unlike semiconductor quantum dots (QD) and are simple to synthesize [6,7]. Within this study we have incorporated novel fluorescent carbon quantum dots (QCD) into a ZnO stabilised hydrogel matrix for selective and sensitive sensing of hexavalent chromium (Cr6+).

Hexavalent chromium is an anionic-charged species of chromium which exists in water as HCrO4 −, HCrO4 2− or Cr2O7 2− depending on pH and concentration. These species are listed as priority pollutants due to their high potential for mobility in aqueous media along with their mutagenic, carcinogenic and teratogenic properties [8]. Numerous industrial activities result in the production of Cr6+ waste including metallurgical and refractory industries, chrome plating, cement industry, fuel combustion, corrosion inhibition and pigments manufacturers [9]. Risks of hexavalent chromium exposure from industrial release are widespread with approximately 170,000 tons of chromium containing wastewater being discharged annually [10]. The early detection of its presence through sensitive sensing applications is of paramount importance to ensure the implication of preventative measures so that serious environmental and human health problems emerge.

Current approaches for determining hexavalent chromium in environmental samples (EPA method 218.7) rely on the use of sophisticated laboratory based equipment i.e. ion chromatography (IC) combined with a guard column, to remove hydrophobic organics and an analytical column with a detection limit of 0.006 μg/L [11]. This approach requires site sampling followed by extensive sample processing by trained scientific staff and the use of expensive laboratory equipment. Furthermore, it is not high throughput; sample processing times are lengthy and it is expensive.

Here we report the design of innovative, semi-solid, three-dimensional hydrogel matrices capable of housing fluorescent quantum dots for environmental sensing applications. We report on the capacity of these photoluminescently enhanced hydrogel composites to selectively interact with hexavalent chromium. In addition, we evaluate the limits of Cr6+ detection within environmental water sources and link the modified hydrogels sensing to the maximum permissible limits of Cr6+ in drinking water. The approach can be conducted in 96 well plates, allowing high throughput analysis of multiple samples in a cost-effective manner.

Methanol (99.9%), Zinc chloride (98%) Polyvinylpyrrolidone (Av. Mol. Wt. 360,000), 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (95%), citric acid (99.5%), quinine hemisulphate monohydrate (99–101%), and sodium chloride (99.5%) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

To pure methanol (13.0 mL), zinc chloride (2.0 g) was added. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (1.0 g) was then added and left for 1 h to allow the ZnCl2 solution to absorb into the polymer. The mixture was subjected to hydrothermal treatment within a 25 mL Teflon lined autoclave for 2 h at 200 °C. The resulting solid pellet was suspended in ultrapure water (18 MΩ) and put in a shaking incubator. The water was replaced every 5 h for 25 h and then once a day for a week to remove unreacted precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water holding capacity and re-swelling capacity in saline solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
The residual water from the metallo-hydrogels was removed by touching sides of gel with tissue paper. The gel was then cut into cubes (∼1 cm3 = ∼1.0 g). The cubes were placed into crucibles and put into an oven at 80 °C. The weight of the gels was recorded periodically until there was no further change in mass. These hydrogel cubes were then re-suspended in NaCl solution (1.0 M, 0.1 M, 0.01 M and 0.0 M NaCl) and their weights were measured periodically until swelling had stopped.
Swelling ratio =
swolen hydrogel
(
g
)
− dried hydrogel
( g )
dried hydrogel
( g )

Quantum carbon dot-containing syrup was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis. Citric acid (5.4 g) was dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water (18 MΩ) and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (1.0 g) was added to make a homogenous solution. The solution was transferred to a teflon lined autoclave (25 mL capacity) and heated to 200 °C for 2.5 h with a ramp up of 6 °C/min. Following the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature and the solution was centrifuged at 5000 X G and the supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove any particulate matter. The resulting solution was lyophillised until residual moisture had been removed and an amber syrup remained.

Hydrogel samples were prepared by passing them through a syringe needle and resuspending them in ultrapure water (18 MΩ). The resulting solution was drop coated on a carbon grid and dried under an infrared heat lamp. Morphological characteristics of the particles were visualised using a JEOL 1010 TEM operated at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of oven dried hydrogel (80 °C) were performed using a Phillips XL30 SEM at an operating voltage of 25 kV and a spot size of 5. Particles were sputter coated in gold prior to imagery.

FTIR spectra of dried hydrogel were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Frontier). An average of 16 scans was collected for each measurement with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

The crystallinity of the dried hydrogel was determined by XRD using a Bruker AXS D8 Discover diffraction instrument equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å) operating at 40 kV and 35 mA. All X-ray data was obtained in the μ-2θ locked-couple mode over a 2θ interval of 5–90°.

The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the QCD’s was determined by comparing the slope of the integrated PL intensity vs maximum absorbance of QCD’s in ultrapure water (18 MΩ) (refractive index η = 1.33) against that of quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index η = 1.33) which possesses a known quantum yield of 54% using the formula:
ΦQCD = ΦQS × (IQCD/IQS) × (ηQCD 2 / ηQS 2)
Where, Φ represents the quantum yield, I is the slope of integrated PL intensity vs maximum absorbance and η is the refractive index. The suffix QS and QCD refer to quinine sulphate and QCD respectively [12,13].

In a typical process, purified swollen metallo-hydrogel (92 g) were soaked in quantum dot solution (0.5 g/L) and allowed to equilibrate for 24 h. Resulting hydrogels (100 mg ± 2.5%) were submerged in ultrapure water (18 MΩ) for 24 h to remove unbound precursors and then passed through a syringe needle into wells of black, flat bottom 96 well plates. The plates were then centrifuged so that surface topographies were even.

To the wells containing hydrogel, 200 μL of different anion and cation solutions made up in ultrapure water (18 MΩ) were added to each well to a final concentration of 2.5 mM. Anions and cations tested included; (Na+, K+, Pb2+, Al3+, PO4 3−, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cs+, Co2+, Cr6+, Fe2+, Fe3+ Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cu2+, Zn2+ and H2O).

Fluorescence and absorbance measurements were conducted on a CLARIOstar® (BMG LABTECH). For fluorescence, maximum excitation and emissions were determined by scanning Milli-Q resuspended samples (200 μL) from 320 to 600 nm. For absorbance measurement, spectral scans between 220 nm and 800 nm were performed.

Fluorescence quenching was determined by subtracting the maximum emissions recorded for cation and anion solution-hydrogel samples from those of hydrogel samples containing water and incorporating these values into the following equation:
F
0
F
− 1
Where F0 and F are the Photoluminescence intensities of the QCD-Hydrogel at 416 nm in the absence (water) and presence of the quencher (Cr6+ solution 2.5 mM) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hexavalent chromium sensing in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: For Cr6+ detection limits within environmental water samples, Cr6+ was serially diluted from 1.25 to 0.0012 mM with centrifuged (but not filtered) river water obtained from the Brisbane Ranges National Park, Victoria, Australia. Hydrogel was prepared into black 96 well plates as previously described, and fluorescence measurements were conducted using the maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fluorescence emissions profiles were measured in triplicate between 360 and 600 nm.

Fluorescence quenching dynamics were quantitatively reinforced by analysing quenching behaviours with synchronous fluorescence quenching ratios plotted against Cr6+ concentration [14]:
F l u o r e s c e n c e
q u e n c h i n g = 1 −
F
F
0
Where F0 and F are the Photoluminescence intensities of the hydrogel/+QCD’s at 416 nm in the absence and presence of the quencher (Cr6+) respectively.

Chemical analysis of QCD-hydrogel with and without Cr6+ was performed using XPS. The instrument used was a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS with monochromated Al 1487 eV K-α source. Charge compensation using a flood gun was required. Peak fitting was performed using the Thermo Scientific Avantage XPS software package. The background signal subtraction of each core peak region of each sample was removed using a Shirley background model.

The gel possessed its own intrinsic fluorescence (Fig. 1); however, we intended to increase these fluorescence capabilities by adding novel QCD’s synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol, a novel amine precursor known for its CO2-absorbing qualities [15]. The intent behind this process was to develop a solid three-dimensional fluorescent matrix for heavy metal ion detection in environmental water samples. Hydrogels have beneficial properties over solid or liquid sensing platforms. They minimise aggregation of quantum dot nanoparticles giving a homogenous sensing platform, prevent self QCD quenching which is often experienced in solid state films and the diffusion of water and its ionic constituents into their matrix is unhindered and rapid [16].

One gram of desiccated hydrogel took 6 days to reach its maximum re-swelling capacity (irrespective of salinity); however, salt concentration had a significant impact on the maximum re-swelling capacity of the gel (Fig. 2B). Freshwater conditions can be categorised by having a NaCl concentration below 0.2 M [17] with sea water possessing a NaCl concentration of approximately 0.6 M [18]. The concentrations of saline water used for re-swelling experiments (1.0 M, 0.1 M and 0.01 M NaCl) span beyond that of sea water and into the freshwater region, giving environmentally relevant concentrations. Hydrogel re-dispersed in saline-free ultrapure water (18 MΩ) possessed a final swelling ratio of 43.34 g/g compared to those subjected to saline water showing swelling ratios of 20.29, 19.03 and 19.35 g/g for 1.0, 0.1 and 0.01 M NaCl concentrations respectively (Fig. 2B). This is a common phenomenon which is a consequence of increased number of anions weakening the electrostatic repulsion between COO− groups, resulting in reduced swelling within the polymer network [19].

The high degree of swellability of the hydrogel is partly due to its non-crystalline amorphous structure, as confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. 3 B). Crystalline phases of hydrogel have been shown to interact with water. However, crystalline phases have limited water absorbing qualities; reducing the crystalline phases from the polymeric matrix leads to the formation of pores where water resides leading to increased water absorption and subsequent swellability [20,21]. Huang et al. (2014) showed that using boron to cross link graphene oxide/PVA hydrogels resulted in the freeing of polymer chains bound within the crystalline PVA. This enhanced the hydrogel pore network and increased water absorption capacity [22]. The high-water-content graphene oxide/polyvinylalcohol hydrogel possessed a water/polymer mass ratio ranging from 19 : 1 to 49 : 1. Results from our study showed comparable water to polymer swelling ratios with 43.3 : 1 respectively.

The lack of peaks representative of ZnO in the gels XRD (Fig. 3B) spectra indicates that ZnO did not possess a perfect wurtzitic structure and/or its concentrations were very low. This phenomenon was shown to be true for Banerjee et al. (2012), where ZnO synthesized in methanol showed compromised crystallinity compared to ZnO synthesized in isopropanol or ethanol which possessed perfect wurtzitic structures [23]. The low concentration of zinc (in its oxide form) was determined by XPS and showed concentrations of 1.64–1.7 mg g−1 of dried hydrogel (data not shown).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The PVP polymer acted as a seeding template for generating dispersed ZnO quantum dots (QD) synthesis under the solvothermal synthesis conditions. Transmission electron microscopy of the dried hydrogel showed a highly cross-linked polymer matrix with branched fibrils between 10 and 40 nm in size (Fig. 3A). In addition, the formation of ZnO QD’s within the polymer, as confirmed by representative Zn2p peaks present at 1021.9 and 1044.8 with XPS analysis (S. Fig. 2) and can visualised via TEM (Fig. 3A), is thought to play a role in the stabilization of the matrix [24]. An increase in polymer crosslinking results in a larger surface area for diffusion to take place and an increased capacity for hydrogels to uptake water [25].

The hydrogel matrix was speckled with QD’s between 1 and 8 nm in size with dots of 3–5 nm making up ∼60% of the total sample (data generated from over 150 CD’s). The conversion of Zn2+ to ZnO under hydrothermal or solvothermal conditions is often accompanied by the addition of a strong base (i.e. NaOH or KOH) for hydrolysis [23,26,27]. Within this study, no alkaline agents were added which enabled the formation of a solid hydrogel. Hydroxyl end groups present in PVP give it a weak reducing property. This can be exploited to produce kinetically controlled growth conditions for generating dispersed ZnO nanoparticles using the precursors used within this study.

Hydrothermal treatment of 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol in the presence of citric acid resulted in a highly fluorescent syrup containing QCD’s. A size histogram of QCD’s calculated by measuring more than 200 particles visualised with TEM shows that their diameters range from 1 to 10 nm with close to 50% being between 3 and 4 nm in size (Fig. 4 ). This solution possessed strong photoluminescence properties with a very high quantum yield for QCD’s of 62.5% using quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate as a reference standard (S. Fig. 10). This syrup (0.5 g) was dissolved in 1 L of ultrapure water (18 MΩ) and the hydrogel was added and allowed to equilibrate with the fluorescent QCD’s. The resulting quantum carbon dot loaded hydrogel (QCD-Hydrogel) was then assessed for its capabilities as a 3D sensing platform for different anions and cations.

To assess the enhanced photoluminescence properties of the hydrogel after the addition of QCD’s, their fluorescence profiles were compared. In addition, their ability to selectively identify specific anions or cations via changes in fluorescence intensities was also investigated.

The hydrogel contained its own intrinsic fluorescence with a maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm resulting in an emissions feature at 416 nm. Following the addition of CQD’s to the matrix, the excitation and emissions profiles remained the same, however the photoluminescence intensity of the QCD-Hydrogel was 7.76 times brighter when compared to the hydrogel alone (Fig. 5 A and B).

The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of QCD’s showed that between pH 4–10 there was no significant change in fluorescence; however, at a pH of 2, the fluorescence intensity dropped by ∼32% (S. Fig. 6). This indicates that the optimum pH range for sensing applications is at pH 4 and above. However, the drop in fluorescence intensity at pH 2 still provides a high fluorescence output and may still give valuable outputs. The hydrogel exposed to 1 M HCL and 1 M NaOH retained its composition following 2 weeks of exposure at room temperature, reinforcing that the composite of QCD and hydrogels are able to tolerate wide pH shifts (S. Fig. 6).

To determine the capacity for the QCD-hydrogel to specifically interact with Cr6+ and in turn, alter its fluorescence profile, photoluminescence measurements were conducted in the presence of 18 different anionic and cationic solutions (Na+, K+, Pb2+, Al3+, PO4 3−, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cs+, Co2+, Cr6+, Fe2+, Fe3+ Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cu2+, Zn2+ and H2O). Fig. 6 A shows that the hydrogel without QCD’s exhibited varying fluorescence profiles in the presence of several cations and anions with significant quenching experienced with many of the species examined. Following the addition of QCD’s to the hydrogel, there were no dramatic reductions in fluorescence intensity in the presence of different anions or cations except for that of Cr6+ and to a minor degree, Fe3+, resulting in a system capable of specifically identifying Cr6+ cations (Fig. 6B). Iron, unlike Cr6+ readily forms highly insoluble oxides in oxygenated conditions and therefore, its influence on the sensor in aerobic environmental samples is limited [28,29]. The concentration of iron present in water sourced from natural sources (Brisbane River, Victoria, Australia) has shown not to influence the detection efficiency of Cr6+ as shown in section 3.7.

The ability for a sensing platform to selectively interact with specific ions and not have interferences from other entities is of paramount importance. In this way can quantitative determinations of specific analytes be identified in complex environmentally derived solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of hexavalent chromium in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practicality of the QCD-hydrogel sensing system for real world applications, we assessed its capacity to detect low concentrations of Cr6+ within an environmentally sourced water sample obtained from Brisbane River, Victoria, Australia (S. Fig. 8). Agricultural water use, ecosystem health and drinking water potability are all effected by Cr6+ concentrations, with Cr6+ being listed as a genotoxic cancer-causing agent and is known to generate reactive oxygen species within animals. The maximum permissible concentration for Cr6+ within drinking water in the USA and Australia is set at 100 ppm. Within plants, Cr6+ has been linked with root growth inhibition and reductions in leaf biomass production and reduced efficiency of photosynthesis [30].

For these reasons, and to ensure that Cr6+ could be detected in water containing numerous potential interfering anionic, cationic and organic species, the limit of Cr6+ detection (sensitivity) within environmentally relevant water samples was determined. The water sample contained a low % dissolved oxygen concentration due to the stagnant nature of the water-body. The pH, conductivity and salinity values were quite high for fresh water (S. Table 2); however they fall within the healthy lowland river quality guidelines outlined by the Australian and New Zealand Environment and Conservation Council [31].

Serial dilutions of Cr6+ were made in the environmentally sourced water (Brisbane River, Vic, Australia) in the range of 0–1.25 mM. To 96 welled plates containing the fluorescent QCD-Hydrogel, 100 μL of these spiked water samples were added. A Cr6+ concentration-dependant quenching of QCD-Hydrogel fluorescence can be observed. Fig. 7 A shows a gradual decrease in PL intensity at 416 nm with increasing Cr6+ concentration. It is worth noting that as the water is alkaline, the dominant Cr6+ species will be the chromate anion, CrO4 2− rather than Cr2O7 2− unless conditions become acidic.

Zhang et al. (2017) was able to achieve similar Cr6+ detection limits (1.2 μM) with their cobalt(II)-doped carbon dots in ethanol solution. Although very effective, the use of an ethanol solvent and the potential added toxicity of cobalt(II) may limit the potential uses for such sensor [32].

Within this current study, the minimum detectable Cr6+ concentration was shown to be 1.2 μM (equivalent to 65 ppb). This sensing platform has the potential for real world monitoring applications as its detection limit for Cr6+ falls below the lowest permissible limit (100 ppb) within drinking water.

UV–visible absorption profiles for the hydrogel and QCD-Hydrogel are represented in Fig. 7B. A shoulder observed at 290 nm is found within all samples and is representative of the π-π* electron transition for CC bonds. The QCD-Hydrogel possesses a shoulder at 333 nm which is representative of the n- π* transition of the CO and CN bonds. This λMAX had a slight redshift following addition of Cr6+ indicating the formation of a complex between the hydrogel-QCD and Cr6+. Time resolved fluorescence decay analysis of the QDs and found that there was a minor Cr6+ concentration-dependant decrease in fluorescence lifetimes. However, at concentrations relevant to this study (Cr6+ concentrations < 50 ppm) the difference in fluorescence lifetimes was negligible (a fraction of a nanosecond) and therefore, energy transfer is not considered to a be a dominant mechanism for fluorescence quenching (data not shown).

The absorption spectrum of Cr6+ overlaps both the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrums of the hydrogel containing quantum dots indicating that fluorescence quenching is due to an inner filter effect (IFE). However, due to the redshift in the λMAX of the QCD-Hydrogel following Cr6+ exposure, a static quenching mechanism is also being experienced.

Further evidence of dual quenching mechanisms, and verification of the QCD-Hydrogels notable sensing sensitivity, can be observed in the linear fluorescence quenching plots for the QCD-Hydrogel (Fig. 7C & D). Quantifiable fluorescence quenching plots show two regions of linearity indicating different quenching processes. The linear range of the sample with Cr6+ concentrations between 0 and 4.9 μM possesses a linear regression with a R2 value of 0.992 (Fig. 8 C) whilst more concentrated Cr6+ concentrations (9.8–625 μM) possess a different linear regression with a R2 value of 0.995 (Fig. 7D). Ding et al. (2018) proposed that this may be due to the shift from monolayer adsorption of Cr6+ onto the QCD-Hydrogel in low quencher concentrations to a multilayer adsorption phenomenon experienced at higher concentrations [33]. XPS analysis of QCD-hydrogel saturated with Cr6+ shows the presence of two different Cr2p oxidation states of chromium (S.Fig. 2). A dominant peak at 579.05 eV is representative of Cr6+ species whereas peaks at 575.89, 576.9, 577.68, 578.68 and 579.09 eV correspond to the reduced and less toxic Cr3+ species. Freeze dried QCD’s in the presence of K2Cr2O7 showed an army-green colour indicating a transformation from Cr6+ to Cr3+ species.

Binding of hexavalent chromium to the citric acid formed dots functionalised with aminopropanol may occur through reactions with the carbon core, present amines and the propanol groups. Kretschmer et al. 2019 has shown that hematite nanoparticles functionalised with citrate, the conjugate base of citric acid, reduce Cr6+ to Cr3+ and form complexes [34]. Hexavalent chromium also reacts as a lewis acid with pyridines resulting in complex formation. XPS analysis of the QCD-hydrogel shows pyridinic-N present in the sample. In addition, Cr6+ is commonly used as an oxidising agent for alcohols where dichromate ions convert primary alcohols (like 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol) to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids [35]. Reaction of Cr6+ with amines results in the removal of a hydrogen atom from the α-position forming a compound with an oxygen occupation in that position or one containing a carbon nitrogen double bond.

The hydrogel was also examined for its ability to be used as a film without the 96 well construct. Thin slices of the un-purified hydrogel were sliced with a razor blade to ∼0.2 mm thickness before purification and were then equilibrated in QCD solution (S. Fig. 3). These flat platforms were soaked in Cr6+ solutions ranging in concentration of 9.6–0.15 mM and subjected to fluorescence measurements (S. Fig. 4). Concentration dependant quenching was observed and showed two regions of linearity which shows a similar trend to the 96 well plate approach. Although the film thickness may have slight variations due to the manual approach of slicing and the fluorescence measurement platform is not specifically tuned to the modified sample processing, results reinforce that hydrogel films can be used for such sensing applications. Future directed research will aim to optimise the use of thin films for sensing platforms to facilitate easier portable fluorescence measurements in the field. This hydrogel was chosen to allow both 96 well plate hydrogel measurements and film studies.

Deconvolution of XPS peaks relating to C1s, N1s, O1s and Zn2p for the QCD-hydrogel were investigated before and after saturation with Cr6+ (Fig. 8 and S. Fig. 2). The C1s spectra of the QCD-hydrogel composite (Fig. 8A) possessed peaks at 284.4, 285.5, 287.0 and 288.2 eV which are representative of C–C/C–H/CC, C–O/C–N, C = O and N–CO respectively. Following exposure to K2Cr2O7, the peak at 288.19 disappeared indicating the removal of N–CO bonds from the complex.

Oxygen O1s peaks for the QCD-hydrogel were present at 531.00 eV and 532.8 eV (S. Fig. 2) being attributed to C–O and CO respectively. Following Cr6+ exposure, the QCD-hydrogel possessed bands at 530.3 and 532.3 eV representative of metal oxide lattice oxygen and CO bonds. Numerous other oxygen containing groups are likely present i.e. C–O, O–CO, N–C–O, CrOx however, the lack of readily identifiable shoulders prevented accurate curve fitting of these species.
QCD-hydrogel before and after Cr6+ exposure respectively showed N1s peaks at 399.3 and 399.1 eV respectively (S. Fig. 2) which are representative of pyridinic-N [36].

The conversion of zinc chloride to ZnO nanoparticles during the solvothermal treatment can be seen by various bonds over the O1s, N1s and Zn2p spectra (S. Fig. 2). The oxygen peak located at ∼530.3 eV has been previously identified as the oxygen in ZnO [37] and zinc peaks at 1022.3 eV and 1044.5 eV are representative of ZnO bound to polymers.

FTIR analysis of the QCD-Hydrogel compared to its organic precursor (PVP) showed remarkable similarities with no observable deviation in bonds following hydrothermal synthesis in the presence of the methonolic zinc electrolyte solution (S. Fig. 1). Due to the dominant peaks in this sample, FTIR investigation into influence of Cr6+ on QCD’s bonds was conducted without the hydrogel matrix.

Hydrothermal conditions with aqueous amine and citric acid precursors facilitate the formation of amides between –NH2 and –COOH [38]. Peaks representative of primary/secondary amides are present within the QCD’s with a N–H stretch bands between 3580 and 3349 cm−1, double CO peaks located at 1699 and 1658 cm−1, C–N stretch feature at 1453 cm−1 and out of plane N–H bending found at 730 and 673 cm−1. Citric acid amides alone have been linked with negligible fluorescence however, their condensation through dehydration at temperatures above 150 °C result in the formation of fluorescent pyridinic carbon dots containing carboxylic acid and amide groups [36,39–41].

A reduction in the presence of primary/secondary amides is shown with QCD’s subjected to Cr6+ by a decreased intensity of the CO band located at 1699 cm−1 relative to the CO band at 1658 cm−1. This is further supported in the XPS spectra with the presence of the N–CO functional group only present in the QCD-hydrogel sample before Cr6+ saturation. Considering the citric acid precursor, the presence of carboxylic acids is also likely to be present. Representative peaks relating to carboxylic acids like citric acid overlap those of amides due to their similar chemical structure and are found within this spectra at OH stretch at 3433 cm−1 CO stretch at 1699 and 1658 cm−1 and C–O stretch at 1172 cm−1.

Within this study, a novel ZnO-QCD-hydrogel with remarkable water absorbing properties (water/polymer mass ratio of 43.3 : 1) has been synthesized in a one-step solvothermal approach. In addition, synthesis of QCD’s using the novel 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol precursor, resulted in particles with great photoluminescence, possessing a quantum yield of 62.5%. When the hydrogel and QCD’s were combined together, a three-dimensional sensing platform was created offering benefits over liquid and solid-state platforms. Benefits include the minimisation of QCD aggregation, prevention of self-QCD quenching and allows for the rapid diffusion of water and ionic species allowing unhindered interaction with the fluorescent matrix. The QCD-Hydrogel showed greatly enhanced photoluminescence compared to the hydrogel without modification. This QCD-Hydrogel composite showed selective fluorescence quenching in the presence of Cr6+ unlike its unmodified counterpart, which showed deviating photoluminescence profiles in the presence of different ionic species. The QCD-Hydrogel was able to detect Cr6+ species down to a 1.2 μM concentration in environmentally sourced water. This is below the maximum permissible concentration for Cr6+ within drinking water in the USA and Australia. These results are encouraging for the development of hydrogels and polymetric films to be used as novel fluorescent sensing platforms.
Adam Truskewycz.
Performed most of experiments, interpreted the data and wrote the manuscript.
Sabrina A. Beker.
Aided with synthesis of QD’s and measurements of Cr6+ sensing experiments.
Andrew S. Ball.
Provided experimental outline advice, corrected manuscript for inconsistencies and aided with the interpretation of results.
Billy Murdoch.
Conducted XPS analysis and interpreted the results.
Ivan Cole.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: 1. The authors acknowledge the facilities, and the scientific and technical assistance of the RMIT Microscopy & Microanalysis Facility (RMMF), a linked laboratory of Microscopy Australia.
2. The authors acknowledge the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for providing the PhD scholarship to Sabrina Beker (co-author).
